title: Performance Issues
description: Practice identifying network performance issues including bandwidth bottlenecks, latency, jitter, packet loss, QoS configuration, broadcast storms, congestion, and performance optimization techniques.
starterCode:
# PERFORMANCE ISSUES EXERCISE

## Part 1: Bandwidth and Throughput

1. _______ is the maximum theoretical data transfer rate of a network link, measured in bits per second.
2. _______ is the actual amount of data successfully transferred, which is always less than bandwidth.
3. A bandwidth _______ occurs when a slower link constrains the overall data flow of the network path.
4. Network _______ occurs when traffic volume exceeds the available bandwidth capacity.
5. _______ shaping controls the rate of traffic flow by delaying excess packets to smooth out bursts.
6. Traffic _______ drops or marks packets that exceed a defined rate limit rather than delaying them.
7. A network _______ test measures the actual data transfer speed between two endpoints.

## Part 2: Latency and Delay

8. Network _______ is the time delay for a packet to travel from source to destination.
9. RTT stands for _______ Trip Time, measuring the time for a packet to go to a destination and return.
10. _______ delay is caused by the physical medium (copper, fiber, wireless) and the distance traveled.
11. _______ delay occurs at each network device (router, switch, firewall) as it processes the packet.
12. _______ delay is the time spent waiting in a device's output queue during periods of congestion.
13. _______ delay is the time needed to push all bits of a packet onto the transmission medium.
14. High latency significantly impacts real-time applications like _______ (Voice over IP) and video conferencing.

## Part 3: Jitter and Packet Loss

15. _______ is the variation in delay between consecutive packets in a data stream.
16. High jitter causes _______ quality degradation in voice and video streams, creating choppy audio or video.
17. A jitter _______ temporarily stores incoming packets to smooth out timing variations before playback.
18. Packet _______ occurs when transmitted packets fail to reach their destination.
19. Packet loss greater than _______% typically causes noticeable quality issues in VoIP calls.
20. Common causes of packet loss include network congestion, faulty _______, and buffer overflows.
21. _______ errors occur when data is corrupted during transmission, requiring retransmission.

## Part 4: Quality of Service (QoS)

22. QoS stands for Quality of _______, a set of techniques to manage network traffic priorities.
23. QoS _______ identifies and categorizes traffic types to apply appropriate handling policies.
24. DSCP stands for Differentiated Services Code _______, used to mark packet priority in the IP header.
25. _______ queuing ensures that high-priority traffic (like voice) is transmitted before lower-priority traffic.
26. Class of Service (CoS) uses the 802.1_______ VLAN tag to mark frame priority at Layer 2.
27. _______ Fair Queuing (WFQ) distributes bandwidth proportionally among different traffic flows.
28. A QoS _______ defines the rules for how different types of traffic should be handled on the network.

## Part 5: Broadcast Storms and Network Issues

29. A _______ storm occurs when broadcast frames loop through a network, consuming all available bandwidth.
30. Broadcast storms are prevented by _______ Tree Protocol (STP), which eliminates Layer 2 loops.
31. A network _______ is a device or link failure that causes a complete loss of connectivity for a segment.
32. _______ exhaustion occurs when a network device runs out of processing power or memory resources.
33. CRC (Cyclic Redundancy _______) errors indicate data corruption, often caused by faulty cables or interfaces.
34. _______ collisions occur frequently on half-duplex links, reducing effective throughput.
35. Interface _______ and resets indicate physical layer problems or configuration issues on a network port.
36. Network _______ analysis examines traffic flow patterns over time to identify performance trends.
37. A performance _______ establishes normal operating parameters against which anomalies can be detected.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Bandwidth and Throughput

1. **Bandwidth** is the maximum theoretical data transfer rate of a network link, measured in bits per second.
2. **Throughput** is the actual amount of data successfully transferred, which is always less than bandwidth.
3. A bandwidth **bottleneck** occurs when a slower link constrains the overall data flow of the network path.
4. Network **congestion** occurs when traffic volume exceeds the available bandwidth capacity.
5. **Traffic** shaping controls the rate of traffic flow by delaying excess packets to smooth out bursts.
6. Traffic **policing** drops or marks packets that exceed a defined rate limit rather than delaying them.
7. A network **speed** test measures the actual data transfer speed between two endpoints.

## Part 2: Latency and Delay

8. Network **latency** is the time delay for a packet to travel from source to destination.
9. RTT stands for **Round** Trip Time, measuring the time for a packet to go to a destination and return.
10. **Propagation** delay is caused by the physical medium (copper, fiber, wireless) and the distance traveled.
11. **Processing** delay occurs at each network device (router, switch, firewall) as it processes the packet.
12. **Queuing** delay is the time spent waiting in a device's output queue during periods of congestion.
13. **Serialization** delay is the time needed to push all bits of a packet onto the transmission medium.
14. High latency significantly impacts real-time applications like **VoIP** (Voice over IP) and video conferencing.

## Part 3: Jitter and Packet Loss

15. **Jitter** is the variation in delay between consecutive packets in a data stream.
16. High jitter causes **audio** quality degradation in voice and video streams, creating choppy audio or video.
17. A jitter **buffer** temporarily stores incoming packets to smooth out timing variations before playback.
18. Packet **loss** occurs when transmitted packets fail to reach their destination.
19. Packet loss greater than **1**% typically causes noticeable quality issues in VoIP calls.
20. Common causes of packet loss include network congestion, faulty **hardware**, and buffer overflows.
21. **CRC** errors occur when data is corrupted during transmission, requiring retransmission.

## Part 4: Quality of Service (QoS)

22. QoS stands for Quality of **Service**, a set of techniques to manage network traffic priorities.
23. QoS **classification** identifies and categorizes traffic types to apply appropriate handling policies.
24. DSCP stands for Differentiated Services Code **Point**, used to mark packet priority in the IP header.
25. **Priority** queuing ensures that high-priority traffic (like voice) is transmitted before lower-priority traffic.
26. Class of Service (CoS) uses the 802.1**p** VLAN tag to mark frame priority at Layer 2.
27. **Weighted** Fair Queuing (WFQ) distributes bandwidth proportionally among different traffic flows.
28. A QoS **policy** defines the rules for how different types of traffic should be handled on the network.

## Part 5: Broadcast Storms and Network Issues

29. A **broadcast** storm occurs when broadcast frames loop through a network, consuming all available bandwidth.
30. Broadcast storms are prevented by **Spanning** Tree Protocol (STP), which eliminates Layer 2 loops.
31. A network **outage** is a device or link failure that causes a complete loss of connectivity for a segment.
32. **Resource** exhaustion occurs when a network device runs out of processing power or memory resources.
33. CRC (Cyclic Redundancy **Check**) errors indicate data corruption, often caused by faulty cables or interfaces.
34. **Late** collisions occur frequently on half-duplex links, reducing effective throughput.
35. Interface **errors** and resets indicate physical layer problems or configuration issues on a network port.
36. Network **baseline** analysis examines traffic flow patterns over time to identify performance trends.
37. A performance **baseline** establishes normal operating parameters against which anomalies can be detected.
hints: Bandwidth is the theoretical maximum; throughput is the actual transfer rate, always lower due to overhead and congestion.
A bottleneck occurs at the slowest link in the path; traffic shaping delays packets while policing drops them.
Latency types: propagation (distance), processing (device), queuing (congestion), serialization (pushing bits onto wire).
RTT = Round Trip Time; high latency especially impacts real-time applications like VoIP and video conferencing.
Jitter is variation in packet delay; a jitter buffer smooths timing for playback; >1% packet loss degrades VoIP.
CRC errors indicate data corruption; common causes include faulty cables, connectors, or interface hardware.
QoS classification categorizes traffic; DSCP marks priority in IP header; CoS uses 802.1p tags at Layer 2.
Priority queuing serves high-priority first; Weighted Fair Queuing distributes bandwidth proportionally among flows.
Broadcast storms loop traffic endlessly; Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) prevents Layer 2 loops.
Performance baselines establish normal parameters; baseline analysis identifies trends and anomalies over time.

testCases.description: Should correctly explain identifies latency concepts and implementation details
Should correctly explain references jitter concepts and implementation details
Should correctly explain references baseline concepts and implementation details
